name: Spain
tactical_document:
# Spain — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Luis de la Fuente's Spain is the modern heir to tiki-taka, refreshed with vertical wide threats. The midfield is the team's bloodstream — they dictate tempo, rotate constantly, and starve opponents of the ball. Spain won Euro 2024 with 65% average possession, then mixed that with the most direct wide play in world football. Juego de posición with teeth. They arrived as FIFA No. 2, reigning European champions and a heavy favourite; six games in, they have done nothing to soften that billing. De la Fuente named a squad with no Real Madrid players and handed the captaincy to Rodri, his Ballon d'Or-winning pivot.

**The road so far: six wins-or-draws, zero defeats.** Group H winners unbeaten (0-0 Cape Verde, 4-0 Saudi Arabia, 1-0 Uruguay — Baena the winner), 3-0 over Austria in the Round of 32, a 1-0 Round-of-16 win over Portugal settled by Merino's stoppage-time strike that ended Ronaldo's last World Cup, and a **2-1 quarterfinal win over Belgium at SoFi Stadium (Fri 10 Jul)**: Fabián Ruiz opened, De Ketelaere equalized — **the first goal Spain have conceded all tournament** — and substitute **Mikel Merino won it within two minutes of coming on**, turning in the rebound after Lammens spilled Cubarsí's shot. Merino has now won Spain two knockout rounds off the bench. Then came the statement: **a 2-0 semifinal masterclass over France (Tue 14 Jul, AT&T Stadium, Arlington)** — Oyarzabal converted a 22nd-minute penalty after Digne fouled Yamal in the box, Porro side-footed the second in the 58th off a give-and-go with Olmo, and Mbappé, Dembélé and Olise were shut down completely (Mbappé did not manage a shot on target). Ledger: **seven games, six clean sheets, thirteen scored, one conceded.**

**FINAL — Argentina (Sun 19 Jul, MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford).** Reigning European champions v reigning world champions. Argentina came from behind to beat England 2-1 in the other semifinal — Enzo Fernández (85') and a Lautaro Martínez stoppage-time header after Gordon had scored, **both assisted by Messi**, who sits top of the Golden Boot race with 8 goals. Spain's team news is clean: **no suspensions**; Yamal limped briefly at the end of the semifinal but there are no real fears over his availability; Porro carries a minor muscular knock from the France game but is expected to recover in time; Yéremy Pino (shoulder, group stage) remains the tournament's only absentee. De la Fuente is expected to name an **unchanged XI** — the same team that started both the QF and the semifinal. Probable XI (4-2-3-1): **Simón; Cucurella, Laporte, Cubarsí, Porro; Fabián Ruiz, Rodri; Baena, Olmo, Yamal; Oyarzabal.**

## Formation
- Shape: 4-2-3-1 — Rodri + Fabián Ruiz double pivot, Olmo the #10; FBs ultra-high in possession (effectively 2-3-5)
- Role mapping (roster order in `spain.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — Unai Simón (ball-playing keeper, build-up partner)
  - index 1: LB — Marc Cucurella (overlapping, sometimes inverts; left side overload)
  - index 2: LCB — Aymeric Laporte (ball-progressor; pass 17 — the deeper playmaker of the pair)
  - index 3: RCB — Pau Cubarsí (composed young CB, line-breaking passer, steps out to cover; his spilled shot created the QF winner)
  - index 4: RB — Pedro Porro (attacking, quick overlapping full-back, speed 17 / stamina 18; overlaps when Yamal cuts in and whips crosses to the back post — scored the second in the semifinal off a give-and-go with Olmo; minor muscular knock, expected fit)
  - index 5: LDM/#8 — Fabián Ruiz (elegant left-footed pivot partner; pass 17, long-range threat — took Pedri's place vs Belgium and scored; keeps the shirt)
  - index 6: RDM/#6 — Rodri (captain & irreplaceable pivot — pass 19, skill 18 — sets every tempo)
  - index 7: CAM/#10 — Dani Olmo (the advanced creator between the lines; arrives late in the box and shoots — skill 18 / shoot 16; scored the Euro 2024 semifinal winner vs France)
  - index 8: LW — Álex Baena (left-sided creator; skill 17 / pass 17 / shoot 15, drifts inside to combine rather than hugging the byline — Spain's dead-ball deliverer)
  - index 9: CF — Mikel Oyarzabal (link-up #9, drops to combine, holds for runners; clinical finisher, shoot 17; primary penalty taker — buried the semifinal penalty vs France)
  - index 10: RW — Lamine Yamal (inverted right winger; dribble 19 / shoot 17, drifts into the right half-space to curl with his left — Spain's primary creator; scored THAT goal vs France at Euro 2024)
  - bench impact: Pedri (the best on-ball connector in the squad, rotated out for the QF — a tempo change in the _5/_7 slots), Mikel Merino (powerful box-to-box, strength 17 — match-winner in BOTH the R16 and QF off the bench, aerial threat in the _5/_7 slots), Marcos Llorente (athletic RB cover, _4), Gavi (relentless pressing energy), Ferran Torres (orthodox right width, shoot 16) and Nico Williams (direct pace for the _8 slot late on, still building fitness) are the key changes; they take the _4/_5/_7/_8/_10 spatial roles when on

## Style of Play

### Build-up
- From Unai Simón: short to Laporte or Cubarsí every time. Goal-kick to row 1 unless pressed 4v4 high.
- Rodri drops between CBs only when needed (3-2-5 vs high press); otherwise stays as pivot with Fabián to his left.
- Both FBs step into midfield height; Olmo floats between the lines, Fabián arrives late from deep.
- **Possession target ~60-65%** — Argentina will not concede the ball as willingly as France did: Scaloni's block presses in waves, Álvarez and Messi curving onto the CBs, De Paul jumping the pivot. Spain still accepts U-shape passing (CB-DM-CB-DM) to wait for a vertical lane. Patience IS the plan; the one sin is the loose central giveaway that feeds the Messi transition.

### Pressing
- Counter-press (Cruyff/Guardiola school): the moment Spain loses it, the nearest 3 players collapse on the carrier for 6 seconds. **Against Argentina this is survival, not style** — every uncontested loss is a potential ball into Messi's feet between the lines, and from there Álvarez and Lautaro run the channels. If not won back in 6 seconds, FOUL-FREE containment and sprint back into shape.
- Trigger high press: Argentina CB receives with back to play, or a square pass between CBs is in flight. Oyarzabal leads, Baena & Yamal curve runs to lock the FBs, Olmo jumps onto Paredes at the base.

### Defensive shape
- 4-1-4-1 with Rodri shielding (Fabián steps into the line). High line (offside trap) — line ~ 50% of pitch length.
- **The Messi rule**: nobody follows Messi out of the block when he drops — Rodri owns the pocket, and Cubarsí and Laporte NEVER both step to the ball; one always protects the depth run (Álvarez's channel sprints, Lautaro late if he's on). Messi drifts to Argentina's right — Spain's left — so Cucurella tucks and Fabián doubles when he receives facing the goal. The high line is Spain's identity AND Spain's risk; it held Mbappé without a shot on target, and it must now erase the pass Messi plays, not the run.
- Olmo has moderate defensive responsibility; tracking back is shared by the wingers.

### Wide play
- Asymmetric width: **LEFT** Baena (_8) drifts inside off the touchline to combine in the half-space → Cucurella overlaps OUTSIDE him for width. **RIGHT** Yamal (_10) starts wide, drifts into the half-space to cut inside on his left → Porro overlaps outside for the cutback option.
- Half-space combinations: the right-side Yamal-Olmo-Porro triangle is Spain's most dangerous pattern — it produced the semifinal penalty (Yamal fouled by Digne) AND the second goal (Olmo's give-and-go releasing Porro). Against Argentina it doubles as Messi-avoidance: attacks down Spain's right force Messi to defend or leave his side exposed.

### Final third
- Patience first: ~30-40 passes per chance is normal. Wait for the FB overlap or the inside-forward cutback.
- Cutback target: Oyarzabal at the penalty spot, Olmo and Fabián arriving late from deep to attack it.
- Yamal's left-foot half-space curler from the right is the recurring termination; Baena's inswinging delivery from the left and Oyarzabal's near-post finish are the alternatives. Fabián's shot from the second wave (see the Belgium opener) is the new wrinkle.

## Set Pieces
- Corners: Inswingers from Baena on left, Yamal on right. Oyarzabal + Laporte + Cubarsí + Olmo attack near/back posts. Rodri at edge of box for rebounds.
- Direct FKs: Yamal (right, primary), Baena (left/central — lethal dead-ball delivery), Laporte (central, power).
- **Penalty-shootout order (knockout — level after 90/120 mins goes to a shootout, so this order is live):**
  1. **Oyarzabal** — first taker; ice-cold, scored the Euro 2024 final winner and buried the semifinal penalty vs France (penalty 17).
  2. **Yamal** — penalty 16, primary creator with the composure for it.
  3. **Rodri** — captain, penalty 15.
  4. **Olmo** — penalty 14, scored in the Euro 2024 shootout run.
  5. **Fabián Ruiz** — penalty 13, the elegant left foot closes.
  - Note: **Baena is NOT in the shootout five** despite his dead-ball quality — his real-world spot-kick record is poor (penalty 9); he takes free kicks and corners, not penalties.
- Defending: zonal with 2 man-markers on biggest threats. Rodri patrols the edge of the box.

## decide() Decision Priorities
1. When my role is GK: always pass short to a CB; only go long if pressed 4v4 and no FB option.
2. When my `player_id` ends with `_6` (DM — Rodri): if no one is open vertically, pass back to CB; never force; my job is to wait — pass 19 means I am the metronome.
3. When my role is DEF and `player_id` ends with `_2` or `_3` (CB pair — Laporte/Cubarsí) and team has ball: pass to the `_6` player (Rodri) first preference, then to the other CB, then to FB. Avoid long balls. NEVER dribble past halfway with Messi lurking to pick off the giveaway and Álvarez alive on the shoulder.
4. When my role is DEF and `player_id` ends with `_1` or `_4` (FB pair) and team_phase is "attacking": advance to LM/RM height; if my `player_id` ends with `_4` (Porro) and the `_10` player (Yamal) cuts inside, I overlap outside him; if my `player_id` ends with `_1` (Cucurella) and the `_8` player (Baena) drifts inside off the left touchline, I overlap OUTSIDE him to provide the width Baena vacates.
5. When my `player_id` ends with `_5` (LDM/#8 — Fabián Ruiz) and team has the ball: hold the left half-space beside Rodri in first phase, then arrive LATE at the edge of the box in the final third — take the second-wave shot when the cutback comes (shoot 15, see the Belgium opener); when possession is lost, I am the first counter-presser of the pivot.
6. When my `player_id` ends with `_7` (CAM — Olmo) and team has the ball: float between the lines in the central pocket, receive on the half-turn (skill 18); play the give-and-go with `_9`, slip `_10` or `_8` into the half-space, and make the late run to attack cutbacks — I am the midfield goal threat (shoot 16).
7. When my `player_id` ends with `_8` (LW — Baena): receive on the left, then drift INSIDE into the half-space to combine (skill 17 / pass 17) rather than beating the FB outside; give-and-go with `_7`/`_9`, deliver the inswinging cross, or Shoot from the left half-space (shoot 15) — let overlapping `_1` (Cucurella) take the outside width.
8. When my `player_id` ends with `_10` (RW — Yamal): start wide then drift into the right half-space; if the ball comes to my feet, Move inside and Shoot with my left (dribble 19 / shoot 17), OR Pass to overlapping `_4` (Porro) outside me.
9. When my `player_id` ends with `_9` (CF — Oyarzabal): drop 8-10m short to receive, lay off to `_7`, then run in behind for the cutback.
10. When ball is lost: immediate 6-second counter-press. Nearest 3 players Move toward the carrier; if I am the closest, Tackle — but NEVER the last defender: contain, don't dive, when Messi is the carrier in space (he wants the lunge; he assisted both semifinal goals off exactly that).
11. When team_phase is "defending" in mid-block: hold a high line — offside trap is the rule. CBs Move forward in sync the moment opponent plays backward; but the weak-side CB always protects depth against the Argentina channel runner (Álvarez, or Lautaro when he's on) the instant Messi's head comes up.
12. Shoot from outside the box only if my `player_id` ends with `_10`, `_8`, `_7`, or `_5` (Yamal/Baena/Olmo/Fabián) AND there's a clear lane.
13. When in doubt, Hold and recycle. Tempo is a weapon; a cheap giveaway is an Argentina goal.

## Key Player Notes
- **Rodri (idx 6)** — captain and the system. "Rodri looks like Rodri again" was the semifinal verdict — he dictated the France game end to end. Without him, Spain is a different team. Never goes above the halfway line in open play; No. 3 in the shootout order. In the final he owns the Messi pocket.
- **Fabián Ruiz (idx 5)** — took Pedri's shirt for the QF, scored, and was arguably Spain's best player in the semifinal (rated ~8.0); the elegant left-footed pivot partner with genuine long-range threat (pass 17). His late arrivals at the box are Spain's second-wave weapon; he doubles on Messi when the ball enters the left pocket.
- **Lamine Yamal (idx 10)** — Spain's primary creator and most decisive attacker. Inverted right winger with license to drift into the half-space and shoot with his left (dribble 19 / shoot 17); the right-side set-piece taker and No. 2 in the shootout. Won the semifinal penalty (fouled by Digne); limped briefly at full time but no availability concerns. The final is billed as Messi v Yamal — the torch game.
- **Dani Olmo (idx 7)** — the #10 (skill 18 / shoot 16 / dribble 16). Arrives late in the box and shoots — the midfield goal threat, No. 4 in the shootout; created the second semifinal goal with the give-and-go that released Porro.
- **Álex Baena (idx 8)** — the left-sided creative inside-forward, not a touchline burner: drifts into the half-space to combine and delivers Spain's left-side dead balls. NOTE: poor real-world penalty record — a free-kick/corner specialist, NOT a shootout taker.
- **Pedro Porro (idx 4)** — attacking right back (speed 17 / stamina 18) who overlaps outside Yamal and whips crosses to the back post; the right-side width in the 2-3-5 — and now a World Cup semifinal goalscorer (cool side-foot finish, rated Spain's best on the night). Carries a minor muscular knock but is expected to start.
- **Oyarzabal (idx 9)** — clinical, selfless #9 and the No. 1 penalty/shootout taker (scored the Euro 2024 final winner, converted the semifinal penalty with total conviction); links play and finishes the cutbacks.
- **Cubarsí (idx 3)** — composed young ball-playing CB; steps out to break lines. His shot forced the spill for the QF winner; he was central to holding Mbappé without a shot on target in the semifinal. Against Argentina: never step with Laporte simultaneously — one always covers the Álvarez run.
- **Laporte (idx 2)** — the deeper distributing CB; marshalled the semifinal expertly. Positioning discipline against the depth run is his whole evening again.
- **Cucurella (idx 1)** — overlapping LB, sometimes inverts; booked early vs France yet defensively immaculate for the rest of the night. Messi drifts into HIS zone in the final — tuck, delay, never dive.
- **Pedri (bench, _5/_7 when on)** — the best on-ball connector in the squad; rotated out for the QF as Fabián scored. A tempo-controlling change for the last half hour.
- **Mikel Merino (bench, _5/_7 when on)** — the man of the knockouts: match-winner in BOTH the R16 (vs Portugal) and the QF (vs Belgium) off the bench. Powerful, aerial, arrives in the box — the designated game-changer.
- **Nico Williams (bench, _8 when on)** — direct pace for the left late on, still building fitness after the adductor injury.
- **Ferran Torres / Gavi / Llorente (bench)** — orthodox width with a goal threat / pressing energy / athletic RB cover.

## Tournament Mindset
**THE FINAL — one game from history.** Seven played, thirteen scored, ONE conceded, six clean sheets — and a semifinal that was less a victory than a verdict: France, with Mbappé, Dembélé and Olise, held to zero shots on target of consequence, beaten 2-0 by a team that never once looked stretched. The XI picks itself for a third straight knockout game — De la Fuente is expected to change nothing — and the treble is on the table: World Cup on top of Euro 2024 on top of the Nations League. This group has not lost a knockout tie that mattered in three years.

The opponent is **Argentina** at MetLife Stadium — the reigning champions, chasing what only Italy (1934-38) and Brazil (1958-62) have done: retain the World Cup. They are dangerous in a way France never was, because they do not merely wait — Scaloni's side presses, adapts, and above all BELIEVES: 1-0 down to England with five minutes left, they won 2-1 through Enzo Fernández and a Lautaro Martínez header in stoppage time, **both created by Messi**, who leads the Golden Boot with 8 goals and now tops the assist chart too, at 39, in his last World Cup, in the most narratively-armed final imaginable. Respect the story; refuse the script. The decisive battles: (1) **Rodri v Messi in the pocket** — nobody chases him, the block simply denies the half-turn, and Fabián doubles on Spain's left where he drifts; (2) **the high line v the channel runners** — Álvarez (and Lautaro late) attack the depth the instant Messi's head lifts, so the weak-side CB protects the run EVERY time, exactly as against Mbappé; (3) **the last-ten-minutes discipline England lacked** — Argentina's comeback was born of a tiring block and a lunged tackle; Spain's answer is possession itself, plus a bench (Merino, Pedri, Williams) that has already won two knockout ties. And mine the right side again: Yamal-Olmo-Porro produced both semifinal goals and forces Messi to defend.

No suspensions, no confirmed absentees beyond Pino; Yamal's knock and Porro's tightness are noted and expected to clear. If it goes the distance, the shootout five are set — Oyarzabal (who has now scored the biggest penalty of this World Cup), Yamal, Rodri, Olmo, Fabián — and Simón has won Spain shootouts before. The mindset is the identity: keep the ball for 60-65%, make the world champions chase for 90+ minutes at their age and their legs, concede nothing cheap, and finish the era-defining sentence — Nations League, Euro, World Cup. One more controlled evening.
